section.title: Breaking Faith Through Divorce
section.sectionRationale: The third disputation turns from the altar to the home. The men of Judah were breaking faith with the wives of their youth and marrying pagan women, profaning the covenant. God exposes this treachery as covenant unfaithfulness that mirrors their unfaithfulness to Him, and He declares His hatred of the violence of divorce.
section.thesis: Because the one God made husband and wife one for the sake of godly offspring, breaking faith in marriage is covenant treachery that God witnesses and hates; faithfulness to one another flows from faithfulness to the one Father.
section.frameNote: One movement in two beats: the charge of treachery, marrying the daughter of a foreign god and profaning the covenant; and the exposure of divorce, where God stands witness for the wife of one's youth and declares that He hates the violence of putting her away.

units.label: One Father, one covenant, broken faith
units.note: treachery against the covenant of the fathers

verses.text: Do we not all have {{echad|one}} Father? Did not one God create us? Why then do we {{bagad|break faith}} with one another so as to {{chalal|profane}} the covenant of our fathers?
Judah has broken faith; an abomination has been committed in Israel and in Jerusalem. For Judah has profaned the LORD’s beloved sanctuary by marrying the daughter of a foreign god.
As for the man who does this, may the LORD cut off from the tents of Jacob everyone who is awake and aware, even if he brings an offering to the LORD of Hosts.

units.label: The wife of your youth
units.note: God witnesses, and God hates divorce

verses.text: And this is another thing you do: You cover the altar of the LORD with tears, with weeping and groaning, because He no longer regards your offerings or receives them gladly from your hands.
Yet you ask, “Why?” It is because the LORD has been a witness between you and the wife of your youth, against whom you have broken faith, though she is your companion and your wife by covenant.

Has not the LORD made them one, having a portion of the Spirit? And why one? Because He seeks godly {{zera|offspring}}. So guard yourselves in your spirit and do not break faith with the wife of your youth.

“For I hate {{shalach|divorce}},” says the LORD, the God of Israel. “He who divorces his wife covers his garment with violence,” says the LORD of Hosts. So guard yourselves in your spirit and do not break faith.

echad.gloss: One, a unity. The same word confesses the one God in the Shema, Deuteronomy 6:4, and here it grounds human faithfulness: one Father, one God, and a husband and wife made one. Because God is one and has made His people one, treachery among them tears at that unity.

bagad.gloss: To act treacherously, to deal faithlessly, to betray. It is the key word of this disputation, repeated of breaking faith with one another, with the covenant, and with the wife of one's youth. It describes covenant betrayal, the opposite of the steadfast faithfulness God shows and requires.

chalal.gloss: To profane, defile, or treat as common what is holy. By breaking faith the men profaned the covenant of the fathers and the LORD's beloved sanctuary. The word marks the seriousness of the sin: it drags something sacred down to the level of the ordinary and unclean.

zera.gloss: Seed, offspring, descendants. God made husband and wife one because He seeks godly offspring, a faithful generation raised in the covenant. The word ties marriage to God's larger purpose of a holy people and, across Scripture, to the promised seed who blesses all nations.

shalach.gloss: To send away, to dismiss, and so to divorce. God declares that He hates the sending away of a wife and calls it covering one's garment with violence. The verse is textually difficult, but on any reading God stands against the treacherous breaking of the marriage covenant.

basic.context: God turns from worship in the temple to faithfulness in the home. The men of Judah were divorcing the wives of their youth and marrying pagan women who served other gods. God calls this treachery, an abomination that profanes the covenant, and He says their tears at the altar are useless while they betray their wives. Then He states plainly that He hates divorce.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage must be read carefully. God's declaration I hate divorce is not a club to crush those already wounded by broken marriages, but a revelation of God's heart against covenant treachery. The verse is one of the hardest in the Old Testament to translate, yet its thrust is clear: God takes the marriage covenant seriously and hates the betrayal that shatters it.
basic.originalLanguages: The key word is bagad, to break faith or betray, repeated again and again. It is because God is echad, one, and has made husband and wife one, that treachery is so grievous. The men chalal, profane, the covenant. God made them one to seek godly zera, offspring. And He hates shalach, the sending away of a wife.
basic.typology: Marriage as a one-flesh covenant points forward to the union of Christ and His church, Ephesians 5. God's faithfulness to the wife of His people's youth, even when they betray Him, foreshadows the unbreakable faithfulness of Christ to His bride, whom He will never divorce.
basic.christ: Israel's treachery against their wives mirrored their treachery against God, yet God remained the faithful husband. That faithfulness is fulfilled in Christ, the Bridegroom who loves His church and gives Himself for her, and who never breaks faith with His people. Jesus also upholds the permanence of marriage, appealing to God's making them one, Matthew 19.
basic.god: God is one, the Father and Creator of His people, and He is a God of covenant faithfulness who witnesses every marriage. He hates treachery and the violence of divorce because He loves faithfulness and the good of families. He is not indifferent to how husbands treat their wives; He stands as witness for the wronged.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2 says the two become one flesh, the foundation Jesus returns to in Matthew 19. Ephesians 5 presents marriage as a picture of Christ and the church. First Peter 3 warns husbands to honor their wives so their prayers are not hindered, echoing the useless tears at the altar here.
basic.oneStory: Malachi ties covenant faithfulness in marriage to covenant faithfulness with God. The betrayal it exposes cries out for the faithful Bridegroom, Christ, who keeps covenant perfectly and gathers a pure bride, restoring the one-flesh union to its true meaning.

context.literary: The third disputation, marked like the others by a divine charge and the people's question, here Why in verse 14. It moves from the community-wide sin of intermarriage with pagans, verses 10-12, to the personal sin of betraying the wife of one's youth, verses 13-16, with the repeated word break faith binding the two together.
context.historical: In the post-exilic community, described also in Ezra 9-10 and Nehemiah 13, Jewish men were marrying women who worshiped foreign gods, often after divorcing their Jewish wives. This threatened the covenant identity and the raising of godly children. Malachi addresses the same crisis the reformers Ezra and Nehemiah confronted, exposing the treachery behind it.
context.canonical: The passage reaches back to Genesis 2:24, the two becoming one flesh, and to the covenant with the fathers. It looks ahead to Jesus's teaching on marriage in Matthew 19, which appeals to God's original design, and to Ephesians 5, where marriage images the union of Christ and His church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the treachery of men breaking faith with their wives and the covenant, to show that such betrayal makes their worship unacceptable, and to call them to guard themselves and remain faithful because God Himself witnesses and hates covenant-breaking.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage prescribes covenant faithfulness in marriage and describes God's hatred of treacherous divorce; it is not a blanket condemnation of every person touched by divorce, nor does it address every circumstance Scripture elsewhere considers. The abiding truth is that God takes the marriage covenant seriously and calls His people to faithfulness.

debates.question: How should the difficult verse 16 be translated and applied?

views.label: God says plainly, I hate divorce
views.case: The traditional rendering has God declaring His hatred of divorce itself as covenant treachery, a strong statement of the seriousness of breaking the marriage bond.

views.label: For the man who hates and divorces his wife
views.case: A well-supported alternative reads it as condemning the man who, hating his wife, divorces her and so covers his garment with violence; the focus falls on the treacherous husband.

debates.bearing: On either reading God stands against the treacherous breaking of the marriage covenant and for the protection of the wronged spouse; the verse must be handled pastorally, not as a weapon against the already broken.

study.originalLanguages: The disputation hammers one word: bagad, to break faith, to betray. Judah has betrayed one another, betrayed the covenant, and betrayed the wife of his youth. The ground of the charge is oneness: we have echad, one, Father, one God made us, and God has made husband and wife one. To betray is to chalal, to profane, the covenant of the fathers and the LORD's beloved sanctuary. God made the two one because He seeks godly zera, offspring, a faithful generation. And He hates shalach, the sending away of a wife, calling it covering one's garment with violence. Verse 16 is famously hard to render, but its force is unmistakable: God is against covenant-breaking treachery in marriage.

typology.type: The one-flesh marriage covenant witnessed by God
typology.fulfillment: The union of Christ and His church, the mystery marriage was made to picture, Genesis 2:24 and Ephesians 5:31-32.

typology.type: God as the faithful husband to an unfaithful people
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Bridegroom who loves and keeps His bride and never breaks faith, Hosea 2:19-20 and Ephesians 5:25-27.
The holy people God gathers in Christ and the promised seed who blesses the nations, Malachi 2:15 and Galatians 3:16.

study.christ: The men of Judah broke faith with their wives just as Israel had broken faith with God, yet the LORD remained the faithful husband to an unfaithful people. That covenant faithfulness is fulfilled in Christ, the Bridegroom who loved the church and gave Himself for her, cleansing her and presenting her to Himself without spot, and who will never divorce His bride. Jesus upholds the very foundation Malachi appeals to, that God made them one, when He teaches on marriage in Matthew 19. The passage that exposes human treachery drives us to the one who keeps covenant perfectly and makes His people faithful.
study.god: God is one, the Father and Creator who has bound His people together and joined husband and wife as one. He is a covenant-keeping God who witnesses every marriage and takes betrayal with utmost seriousness, refusing the worship of those who weep at the altar while wronging their wives. He hates the violence of treacherous divorce because He is faithful and loves faithfulness, and He stands as witness and advocate for the one who has been betrayed.

crossReferences.note: The two become one flesh, the design Malachi appeals to and Jesus returns to in teaching on marriage.
Jesus grounds the permanence of marriage in God's making them one, echoing Malachi's argument.
Marriage images the faithful, self-giving union of Christ and His church, the fulfillment of covenant faithfulness.
The same post-exilic crisis of intermarriage with pagan peoples that Malachi confronts as covenant treachery.

study.oneStory: Malachi binds faithfulness in marriage to faithfulness with God, exposing a people who betray both. This treachery cries out for the faithful Bridegroom, Christ, who keeps covenant where His people fail, loves His bride to the end, and restores the one-flesh union to the glory it was made to display. From the one Father comes the call to be one, fulfilled in the union of Christ and His church.

sermon.title: Malachi's Message On Marriage (Malachi's Modern Message Part 4)
sermon.why: The fourth message of Legge's expository series through Malachi, covering 2:10-16. It handles the treachery of breaking faith, the seriousness of the marriage covenant, and the difficult verse on divorce with pastoral care, an orthodox exposition that points to God's covenant faithfulness fulfilled in Christ the Bridegroom.

quiz.q: What word runs through this whole disputation?

options.text: Breaking faith, or treachery, against one another, the covenant, and the wife of one's youth.
options.note: The Hebrew bagad, to betray, is repeated throughout verses 10-16.

options.text: Sacrifice, the theme of proper offerings.
options.note: Offerings appear, but the driving word is breaking faith, not sacrifice.

options.text: Wealth, the theme of tithing.
options.note: Tithing is the next disputation; this one is about covenant treachery in marriage.

options.text: Idolatry alone, with no mention of marriage.
options.note: Marriage and the wife of one's youth are central here.

quiz.q: Why were the people's tears at the altar not accepted?

options.text: Because they were breaking faith with the wives of their youth even as they wept.
options.note: Verses 13-14 tie the rejected worship to their treachery at home.

options.text: Because they did not cry loudly enough.
options.note: The issue was not the volume of grief but the betrayal behind it.

options.text: Because they brought the wrong kind of grain.
options.note: The problem was covenant treachery, not the offering's ingredients.

options.text: Because God never accepts tears.
options.note: God receives genuine repentance; here the tears masked ongoing betrayal.

quiz.q: Why did God make husband and wife one?

options.text: Because He seeks godly offspring, a faithful generation raised in the covenant.
options.note: Verse 15 gives godly offspring as the purpose behind the oneness.

options.text: Only to provide companionship with no larger purpose.
options.note: Companionship is real, but the verse names godly offspring as the aim.

options.text: To increase the nation's military strength.
options.note: The purpose is godly children, not armies.

options.text: For no stated reason at all.
options.note: God explicitly states the reason: He seeks godly offspring.

quiz.q: How should we handle God's statement that He hates divorce?

options.text: As a revelation of God's heart against covenant treachery, handled with pastoral care.
options.note: The verse exposes betrayal and is not a weapon against the already wounded.

options.text: As permission to look down on anyone who has been divorced.
options.note: It condemns treachery, not the wronged; it is not a license for contempt.

options.text: As proof that marriage does not really matter to God.
options.note: The opposite: it shows how seriously God takes the marriage covenant.

options.text: As an outdated rule with no meaning today.
options.note: It reveals God's abiding heart for faithfulness in marriage.

quiz.q: How does this passage point to Christ?

options.text: God's faithfulness to an unfaithful people is fulfilled in Christ, the Bridegroom who never breaks faith with His bride.
options.note: Ephesians 5 shows marriage picturing Christ's faithful love for the church.

options.text: It teaches that Christ abolished marriage.
options.note: Christ upholds marriage, grounding it in God's making them one.

options.text: It shows that human treachery is acceptable to God.
options.note: God hates treachery; Christ keeps covenant where we fail.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: It points directly to Christ the faithful Bridegroom of His church.

questions: God witnesses every marriage covenant. How does knowing that God Himself stands as witness shape the way you keep your promises to those closest to you?
The people wept at the altar while betraying their wives at home. Where might your worship be undercut by unfaithfulness or broken relationships you have not addressed?
God made husband and wife one for the sake of godly offspring. However God has placed you, how are you investing in raising up a faithful generation who love Him?
God calls His people to guard themselves in their spirit. What practical guardrails help you stay faithful in your commitments when it would be easier to break faith?
Christ is the Bridegroom who never breaks faith with His bride. How does His unfailing faithfulness to you steady and encourage you when you feel your own faithfulness faltering?

translationNotes.note: The BSB prints an em dash before even if he brings an offering; this has been rendered with a comma per house style, keeping the sense that not even an offering will shield the treacherous man from being cut off.
Verse 15 is grammatically difficult in Hebrew; the BSB renders the thrust that God made the couple one and seeks godly offspring, grounding the call not to break faith.

This verse is one of the hardest in the Old Testament to translate. The BSB gives For I hate divorce and carries a footnote offering the alternative, For the man who hates his wife and divorces her covers his garment with violence; on either reading God opposes treacherous covenant-breaking in marriage.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Malachi 2:10-16. All 7 verses reproduced in order. An em dash in verse 12 has been rendered as a comma per house style. The BSB footnote at 2:16 offering the alternative rendering of the divorce clause is reflected in the translation notes and hermeneutics debate.
